title: Photo Restoration Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Photo Restoration Business — Real Costs and Earnings
metaDescription: Honest guide to a photo restoration business: real per-photo pricing, realistic earnings, the AI-tool pressure, and how to find genealogy and memorial clients.
bestFor: Detail-oriented people with solid Photoshop skills who enjoy meticulous, meaningful work and can find clients in genealogy and memorial niches
biggestRisk: Cheap AI restoration tools and low-cost overseas gigs pushing prices down faster than you can build a premium reputation
whatItIs: A photo restoration business repairs and improves old, damaged, or faded photographs for individuals and families — removing scratches, tears, water and mold damage, fading, and creases, rebuilding missing areas, and colorizing black-and-white images. Most clients come to you with deeply personal photos: a torn portrait of a grandparent, a damaged wedding photo, or images for a memorial or family-history project. The work is done in Photoshop (or similar editors) and delivered as digital files and often prints. It's an accessible, low-cost business to start because the main asset is skill, but it sits under real pressure from cheap automated and AI tools, so the durable money is in skilled, hand-done restoration of difficult photos that automation can't handle well.
dailyReality: The work is quiet, focused screen time: scanning or importing a client's photo, then painstakingly cloning, healing, and rebuilding damaged areas pixel by pixel, matching textures and tones, and colorizing where requested. A complex restoration can take from under an hour to many hours depending on damage. Around the editing you handle the client side — discussing what's possible (and gently managing expectations on badly damaged photos), quoting, collecting the original scan, sending proofs, handling revisions, and arranging prints. Because the photos are emotionally important, clear, kind communication is a real part of the job.

startupCostsItemized.item: Photo editing software (Adobe Photoshop subscription or Affinity Photo one-time)
Computer capable of photo editing (use one you own to start)

realEarnings.yearOne: Beginners typically earn $300 to $1,500 per month part-time while building a portfolio and reviews. Early per-photo prices often run $20 to $60 for moderate restorations; income depends on how many clients you can reach and how fast you work. Expect it to be a side income at first.
realEarnings.experienced: Skilled restorers with a portfolio and steady referrals commonly earn $1,500 to $4,000 per month, charging roughly $50 to $200+ per photo depending on damage and colorization, with complex or memorial-project work at the higher end. Bundles for family-history projects raise the average order.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top restorers and small studios serving genealogy organizations, funeral homes, and high-end private clients, or selling courses and presets on the side, can reach $60,000 to $120,000+ a year. Getting there took standout skill on difficult restorations, a strong niche reputation, repeat institutional clients, and efficient workflows — not just cheap volume.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Skill on hard restorations and a reputation in the right niches (genealogy, memorials) matter most, because that's the work cheap AI tools and $5 gigs can't replicate. Speed and clear expectation-setting also drive the real hourly rate, since badly damaged photos can swallow hours.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rate varies with skill and job difficulty. Beginners often net $15 to $30 per hour while learning and over-servicing; experienced restorers commonly reach $40 to $80+ per hour by working efficiently and pricing difficult jobs properly rather than per simple photo.

howToStart: Week 1: Sharpen your core Photoshop or Affinity Photo skills — healing, cloning, frequency separation, masking, and basic colorization. Restore several of your own and friends' old photos to build before/after samples.
Weeks 1-2: Build a simple portfolio that leads with dramatic before/after pairs, and set clear pricing tiers (light, moderate, heavy damage, plus colorization) instead of a single flat rate.

Weeks 2-4: List on freelance platforms and offer restorations in genealogy and family-history communities, local Facebook groups, and Nextdoor. Price early jobs to win, but always collect a testimonial and a before/after for your portfolio.

Months 1-3: Build repeat and referral channels — partner with funeral homes, genealogy societies, photographers, and framing shops who see clients needing restoration. Add print and framing as an upsell.

Months 3-12: Raise prices as your portfolio and speed improve, lean into the harder restorations that AI tools botch, and consider adding services like colorization specialization, large family-history projects, or teaching.

mustHave: Solid Photoshop or Affinity Photo skills — cloning, healing, masking, and tone/color matching
Strong attention to detail and patience for meticulous, pixel-level work
Honest expectation-setting on what can and can't be recovered from badly damaged photos
Kind, clear communication, since clients are entrusting you with irreplaceable, emotional photos
canLearn: Colorization of black-and-white images to realistic, period-appropriate tones
Advanced reconstruction of missing faces and areas, and texture matching
Scanning best practices and print/output color management
separators: Skill on severe damage and colorization that automated tools handle poorly
A reputation and referral base in genealogy and memorial niches
Efficient workflow and proper pricing so difficult jobs stay profitable

whatPeopleGetWrong: Competing only on price against cheap AI tools and $5 gigs instead of specializing in difficult, hand-done restoration
Over-promising on severely damaged photos, then disappointing clients who expected a miracle
Under-pricing complex jobs that take hours, leaving a poor effective hourly rate
Delivering over-processed, plastic-looking results from heavy AI filters rather than natural restoration
Working from low-quality phone snapshots of originals instead of requiring a proper high-resolution scan
Neglecting the emotional, communication side of work involving irreplaceable family photos

toolsAndEquipment.note: Industry standard for restoration; subscription cost but unmatched retouching tools.
Capable one-time-purchase alternative to Photoshop for restoration work.
Greatly improves precision and speed for detailed retouching. A small used tablet works.
For high-quality scans of physical originals; some clients send their own scans.
Matters for accurate color, especially for colorization and prints.
Offering prints and framing raises order value; a local lab works to start.

methods: A portfolio of striking before/after restorations on a simple site and on freelance platforms
Genealogy and family-history communities, where restoring ancestor photos is a recurring need
Partnerships with funeral homes, genealogy societies, photographers, and framing shops
Local Facebook groups and Nextdoor, especially around holidays, anniversaries, and memorials
Asking happy clients for reviews and referrals, since restoration is highly word-of-mouth driven
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Individuals and families with damaged or faded photos — concentrated in genealogy and family-history circles, people preparing memorials, and those digitizing inherited photo collections. Demand rises around holidays, anniversaries, and after a death in the family.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Most restorers land first paid jobs within one to four weeks of having a portfolio, with a steady referral-fed flow typically taking three to six months of building reviews and niche partnerships.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad paid advertising to the general public converts poorly for this emotional, trust-based purchase. Early effort is far better spent on a strong before/after portfolio, reviews, and partnerships with funeral homes, genealogy groups, and photographers.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, but it requires either steady volume or premium positioning, since per-photo prices are modest. Full-time income comes from a full pipeline of referral and repeat work, higher-value colorization and project jobs, and add-ons like prints — bounded by how many hours you can edit.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible by training other retouchers and routing overflow, but quality and the personal client relationship are hard to delegate fully. Many stay solo; some add income by teaching restoration or selling presets and tutorials rather than hiring.
howItScales.sellable: Modestly sellable. A studio with a brand, repeat institutional clients (funeral homes, genealogy societies), documented processes, and possibly digital products has some transferable value, but a pure solo practice is mostly the owner's skill and relationships.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: An efficient workflow, reliable trained help for overflow, repeat and institutional client relationships, and ideally additional income from courses, presets, or premium project work that isn't strictly per-hour.

strongFit: You have or can build strong Photoshop skills and enjoy meticulous, detailed work
You find meaning in preserving people's irreplaceable family photos
You want a low-cost, part-time-friendly business you can run from home
You're willing to specialize in difficult restoration that automated tools can't match
poorFit: You want high prices for simple jobs or expect to compete purely on volume
You find slow, detailed retouching tedious or frustrating
You're uncomfortable managing emotional clients and setting honest expectations
You're unwilling to keep improving as AI tools absorb the easy, low-end work
askYourself: Are my restoration skills good enough on hard photos to justify charging more than a cheap AI tool?
Can I build referral relationships in genealogy and memorial niches where this work lives?
Will modest per-photo prices and detailed work pay off enough for the hours involved?

faqs.question: How much can I charge to restore a photo?
faqs.answer: Pricing is usually per photo and scales with damage. Beginners often charge $20 to $60 for light to moderate restoration; experienced restorers charge $50 to $200+ for heavy damage, reconstruction, or colorization. Family-history bundles and memorial projects raise the average order. Price difficult jobs by the work involved, not a flat per-photo rate, or hard restorations will be unprofitable.

faqs.question: Do I need to be a Photoshop expert?
faqs.answer: You need solid, not world-class, Photoshop (or Affinity Photo) skills to start — healing, cloning, masking, and basic colorization will handle most jobs. Expertise on severe damage and reconstruction is what lets you charge more and take work cheap competitors and AI tools can't. You can learn as you go, but you can't fake the core skills.

faqs.question: Are AI photo restoration tools going to kill this business?
faqs.answer: They've genuinely raised pressure on the low end — automated tools can do quick, simple cleanups cheaply, which has pushed down prices for basic work. They struggle with severe damage, missing areas, natural-looking results, and tasteful colorization, which is where skilled human restorers stay valuable. The honest strategy is to specialize in the difficult, high-trust work automation handles poorly, and you can even use AI as a starting point you then refine by hand.

faqs.question: Where do I find photo restoration clients?
faqs.answer: Genealogy and family-history communities, funeral homes, photographers, and framing shops are strong niches, alongside local Facebook groups and freelance platforms. Demand clusters around holidays, anniversaries, and memorials. Because the photos are personal, this business is heavily word-of-mouth, so reviews and referrals matter more than advertising.

faqs.question: How do I handle a photo that's too damaged to fully restore?
faqs.answer: Set expectations honestly before taking payment. Explain what's realistically recoverable, show a quick sample if needed, and quote accordingly. Clients deeply appreciate honesty about an irreplaceable photo, and over-promising on a badly damaged image is a fast route to refunds and bad reviews.

faqs.question: Can I really do this part-time around a job?
faqs.answer: Yes — it's flexible, low-cost, and largely asynchronous, so it suits evenings and weekends. The main constraint is that complex restorations can take hours, so manage your queue and turnaround promises. Many people build a portfolio and first clients part-time before deciding whether to scale up.

Photo restoration studio and specialist pricing pages and case studies
